title: Cat Hair Loss and Grey Scaly Skin: Causes and Next Steps
meta_desc: Learn about the common causes of feline hair loss and grey scaly skin, from ringworm to allergies, and find out if it requires an emergency vet visit.
content_html:
What is it?

Noticing hair loss combined with grey, scaly patches on your cat's skin can be alarming, but it is a common symptom of several dermatological issues. These patches are often the result of an accumulation of dead skin cells or a thickening of the skin due to chronic irritation. The most frequent causes include fungal infections such as ringworm, parasitic infestations like mites (mange), or chronic inflammatory conditions like feline atopy or food allergies.

Is it an emergency?

Generally, hair loss and scaly skin are considered low-urgency medical issues. While these symptoms cause discomfort and itching for your cat, they are rarely life-threatening. You should schedule a routine appointment with your veterinarian to get an accurate diagnosis. However, if you notice the skin is bleeding, oozing, or if your cat becomes lethargic and stops eating, you should seek veterinary care more promptly.

How a photo helps triage

Providing a high-quality photo of the affected area is one of the most helpful tools for a veterinary triage team. A clear image allows us to examine the pattern of hair loss and the specific texture of the scales. For instance, circular patches might suggest ringworm, while localized scaling on the ears might point toward mites. This visual data helps your vet determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen and which diagnostic tests to prepare.

faqs.question: Can I catch skin conditions from my cat?
faqs.answer: Yes, some conditions like ringworm are zoonotic, meaning they can spread from animals to humans. It is important to practice good hygiene and wash your hands after touching the affected areas.

faqs.question: Should I try an over-the-counter antifungal cream?
faqs.answer: You should avoid applying human medications to your cat. Cats frequently groom themselves and may ingest creams that contain ingredients toxic to them. Always wait for a veterinarian's recommendation.

faqs.question: Will my cat's hair grow back?
faqs.answer: In most cases, once the underlying cause of the scaling and inflammation is treated, the hair follicles will recover and the fur will grow back over time.
